MARKET.head: Tequila auction record fuels investment boom speculation
MARKET.summ: With tequila hitting a new world auction record in recent weeks, and WineFi offering irs first premium tequila investment barrels, is the agave spirit set for a new boom? Arabella Mileham reports
takeaways: Clase Azul bottle sold for US$35,000 at Sotheby's, setting new tequila auction record and first spirits top lot.
Global tequila market projected to grow from US$12.6bn to US$21.6bn by 2033, driven by younger premium consumers.
WineFi launched premium tequila investment fund with 50 barrels, citing historic returns of 15–35% on comparable aged assets.
Extra Añejo tequila requires minimum three years maturation; investment funds recommend ten-year holding periods due to supply constraints.
Sotheby's spirits head noted whiskey drinkers increasingly switching to agave spirits, marking potential new collectible category.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, English wine pioneer estate, sells after 52 years
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Sussex vineyard founded by Peter Hall in 1974 listed for £4 million following his death in October 2025.
Estate produces around 10,000 bottles annually across approximately six acres, specializing in sparkling wines from Seyval Blanc and classic varieties.
Hall's wines served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and 2012 London Olympics.
English wine sector has grown from seven vineyards in 1974 to over 1,300 today; 2025 production rose 39% year-on-year.
Sale includes 15-acre property with farmhouse, winery barn, and full vineyard operations; Knight Frank expects international buyer interest.

MARKET.head: MacNeil examines prestige and pricing tensions in Bordeaux and Napa Valley
MARKET.summ: By Karen MacNeil July 10, 2026 Originally 3/18/2026 (A version of this piece originally appeared in Decanter magazine in May 2026.) The Wine Bible author Karen MacNeil analyzes the paradox of Bordeaux and Napa Valley, which are simultaneously revered for producing great wines and vilified by industry insiders due to high prices and elite
takeaways: Bordeaux and Napa Valley face a paradox: celebrated for quality yet criticized by industry insiders for high prices and exclusivity.
Wine Bible author Karen MacNeil explores the disconnect between consumer reverence and insider skepticism toward these regions.
Elite positioning of both regions creates tension within wine industry attitudes toward their market dominance.

MARKET.head: Japanese winery awards boost visibility, but exports remain limited to larger producers
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: International awards like DWWA and IWSC have recognized Japanese wines since 2010, with Suntory's Koshu winning DWWA Best in Show in 2024.
About 70 percent of Japan's 400-plus wineries are family-owned operations too focused on domestic production to pursue exports.
Larger producers like Château Mercian and Katsunuma Jyozo credit awards with providing commercial credibility and enabling export deals.
Restaurant placement and high-profile venues drive sales more effectively than awards alone, according to importers and producers.
High production costs from Japan's terrain and weather, plus premium pricing, limit export appeal despite growing international recognition.

MARKET.head: Bordeaux back vintages lead H1 fine wine gains amid disciplined buying
MARKET.summ: The first half of the year marked "a turning point" for investors of fine wine, a recent Q2 report from WineCap has said. With Bordeaux back vintages dominating, tit proves that the fine wine market is increasingly rewarding "disciplined buying”
takeaways: Nine of top ten performing wines in H1 were Bordeaux back vintages, led by Château Climens 2012 up 66.1%.
Burgundy, Tuscany, Rhône, Napa Valley and Chile featured prominently in top 20, signaling broad secondary market recovery.
Investors shifted toward established brands offering value relative to quality, avoiding momentum-chasing in en primeur campaign.
2025 vintage presented strong entry point, but campaign exposed Bordeaux's need to realign new-release pricing with buyer expectations.
Relative value and disciplined selection now define fine wine market as stable prices persist amid broader macro uncertainty.

CULTURE.head: English wine production surges 55% in 2025, reaching 16.5 million bottles
CULTURE.summ: Following yesterday's 2026 rankings from The Real Review's 'Top Wineries of Great Britain' tasting, in which all of the top 30 wines hailed from England, Anthony Rose analyses the current state of UK wine
takeaways: UK wine production hit 124,377 hectolitres in 2025, up 55% from 2024, with white wine surging 131%.
Quality metrics improved: growing degree days reached 1,051, potential alcohol rose to 10.48%, and acid levels declined slightly.
Still wine now accounts for growing share of production; Essex's Crouch Valley emerging as heartland for English Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
Industry expanded to 1,158 registered vineyards and 4,357 hectares in production, employing over 10,000 people and valued at £14 billion.
Fungus-resistant grape varieties like Solaris gaining traction for sustainability; export volumes grew 35% in 2024 to reach 9% of sales.

CULTURE.head: Asia's drinks industry convenes for education, trade events in June
CULTURE.summ: From Hong Kong to Tokyo, Asia's drinks industry came together in June through education, tasting experiences and major trade events, as mixologists, sommeliers and sake makers celebrated craftsmanship, and explored new opportunities for growth
takeaways: The Bitter Truth founder hosted a masterclass in Hong Kong for bartenders as part of the brand's 20th anniversary Asia Tour.
Wines of Hungary held its inaugural Bor Camp in Seoul on June 23-24, bringing sommeliers and buyers together to explore Hungarian wine regions and varietals.
Tokyo's Sake Fair returned June 19-20 with 5,600 visitors and around 1,200 sakes from 45 prefectures, plus 400 award-winning selections in a public tasting.
Lemsecco launched in China with lemon and blood orange spritz varieties stocked in over 400 Freshippo stores and 10 premium supermarket chains.
Summer activations across Asia included in-store tastings, branded promotions, and partnership events designed to engage young urban consumers.

CULTURE.head: London masterclass explores diverse rosé styles and producers
CULTURE.summ: Think you might be getting stuck in a rosé rut? Now you can find a pink wine that fits your present mood or preferred food at an evening masterclass in London on 10 September
takeaways: Rosé tasting masterclass scheduled for September 10 at The Chelsea Townhouse in London, starting at 6pm.
Event hosted by Master of Wine Patrick Schmitt, limited to 28 guests, featuring niche and mainstream rosé brands.
Masterclass designed for all experience levels, from casual drinkers to knowledgeable wine enthusiasts.
Part of the What to Drink series by trade publication the drinks business, offering producers direct contact with consumers.

CULTURE.head: Pol Roger releases 2019 Champagne from challenging but exceptional vintage
CULTURE.summ: Champagne Pol Roger has unveiled its latest vintage expression from the exceptional 2019 harvest, and Patrick Schmitt MW gives his verdict
takeaways: 2019 vintage experienced April frost, mildew, and August hailstorms, reducing regional yields by nearly 20%.
Ideal harvest conditions produced wines with rare harmony, freshness, and aromatic richness, according to Pol Roger.
2019 achieved average alcohol of 10.7%, higher than celebrated 2018 vintage at 10.2%.
Blend contains 63.5% Pinot Noir and 36.5% Chardonnay from premier and grand cru vineyards; aged six years.
Retails for £365 per bottle, 10% cheaper than 2018 release; expected to develop complexity over 3-5 years.

CULTURE.head: Sam Neill, Two Paddocks founder, dies at 78
CULTURE.summ: Sam Neill, the actor behind Jurassic Park's Dr Alan Grant and founder of Central Otago winery Two Paddocks, has died aged 78. While celebrated across the world for his film career, he spent more than three decades quietly building one of New Zealand's most respected Pinot Noir estates
takeaways: Actor planted first vineyard in 1993, developing Two Paddocks into Central Otago's benchmark Pinot Noir producer over 30+ years.
Estate expanded across four vineyard sites in Gibbston, Alexandra and Bannockburn, becoming sole Central Otago producer in all three valleys.
Winery achieved organic certification from 2017 vintage, prioritizing estate-grown Pinot Noir and site-specific bottlings over celebrity profile.
Neill rejected 'celebrity winemaker' label, arguing it diminished the work behind the wines and prioritizing quality over fashionable styles.
He championed Burgundian vineyard principles and advocated for New Zealand Pinot Noir recognition alongside Burgundy on global stage.

SCIENCE.head: Castello di Amorosa partners with Water Audit California to study Nash Creek
SCIENCE.summ: July 13, 2026 (Napa, CA) , Castello di Amorosa and Water Audit California today announced a collaboration for the study of Nash Creek, a significant tributary of the upper Napa
takeaways: Castello di Amorosa and Water Audit California announced a collaboration on July 13, 2026.
The partnership focuses on studying Nash Creek, a tributary of the upper Napa River.
The initiative is based in Napa, California.

SCIENCE.head: Grape sunburn spreads beyond hot climates as heat waves intensify
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Sunburn occurs when berry surface temperatures exceed 104-109°F under high light, causing chlorophyll and aromatic compounds to degrade within minutes.
Affected wines display cooked fruit, asphalt, and creosote notes with flattened aromatics, high alcohol, low acidity, and premature aging characteristics.
Mid-to-late summer defoliation now carries risk; producers increasingly delay leaf removal until end of flowering to allow photoprotective compound buildup.
Kaolin clay reflective films reduce canopy temperature by up to 9°C; white varieties now receive minimal or no summer defoliation in affected regions.
Vineyard sorting and canopy architecture are becoming critical defenses as traditional sun-exposure practices prove counterproductive in warming climates.

SCIENCE.head: French heatwave to cut Champagne yields by 10 percent
SCIENCE.summ: France's prolonged summer heat and drought are shrinking grape development across major wine regions, with Champagne growers expecting yields to fall around 10% and harvests beginning weeks earlier than normal
takeaways: Champagne producers forecast yields around 10% lower than last year due to prolonged heat and drought across France.
Champagne harvest expected to begin around August 15, potentially the earliest on record, roughly a month ahead of historical norms.
Burgundy and Bordeaux also experiencing significant heat damage; growers cite water shortage concerns and rapid sugar accumulation in grapes.
Champagne houses may offset yield losses by drawing on reserve wines; final production impact remains uncertain pending rainfall.
Extended dry conditions forecast through mid-July across France's major wine regions with little relief expected in near term.

SCIENCE.head: Heat-Tolerant Mourvèdre Struggles as Southern France Temperatures Soar
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: Mourvèdre, traditionally grown in Bandol, faces balance challenges as rising heat and drought strain even heat-adapted varieties.
Producers adopt canopy management techniques—including higher gobelet training and shade planting—to protect grapes from excessive sun exposure.
Drought pressures prompt debate over dry farming's viability; some regions now consider irrigation essential for vine survival in severe dry years.
Jumilla's vineyard area has halved in 20 years as hotter southern sites are abandoned; cooler high-altitude parcels remain viable.
Bandol's rosé production (75% of output) may decline as cooler sites shift to red wine to adapt to warming conditions.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Video: visiting an agroforestry coffee plantation in Brazil, the 15 hectare Sombra experiment that’s a collaboration between Fazenda Mió and Volcano Coffee Works in the UK
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Coffee is often grown as a monocrop. Here, at Fazenda Mió in Brazil I’m visiting a 15 hectare agroforestry experiment,
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Fazenda Mió in Brazil operates a 15-hectare agroforestry coffee experiment with UK partner Volcano Coffee Works.
Four coffee varieties are interplanted with three tree types to provide shade and enhance soil life.
Cover crops between coffee rows boost soil microbiology and protect soil structure.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Wine News: What I’m Reading the Week of 7/12/26
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hello and welcome to my weekly roundup of the wine stories that I find of interest on the web. I
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alder Yarrow

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Calabria has nine scattered DOCs; only Cirò, in the east, has achieved fame for its red Gaglioppo variety.
Magliocco comes in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (more tannic, common in eastern Calabria) and Magliocco Canino (retains acidity, extremely late ripening).
Casa Comerci, a certified organic estate near Nicotera on Calabria's Tyrrhenian coast, ferments and ages exclusively in stainless steel tanks, never barrels.
Magliocco Canino may be related to Sangiovese and is so late-ripening that Casa Comerci's 2017 rosé was harvested in early December.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Too Difficult?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Yeats entitled one of his mid-life poems ‘The Fascination of What’s Difficult’. The poem is mainly a complaint, about the tangled business of running a theatre (the Abbey in Dublin) and the infuriating difficulty of “management of men”, as opposed to the pure delight of writing poetry. But the title points to something which has
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tim Atkin MW
takeaways: Assyrtiko, Greece's assertive grape variety, divides drinkers with high acidity but rewards those who persevere.
Orange wines by Joska Gravner of Friuli initially baffle tasters lacking conventional fruit and floral notes, but reveal gravitas through repeated tasting.
Nebbiolo-based Piedmont reds challenge expectations with pale color, high tannins, and earthy aromas rather than berry fruit.
Sherry's deliberate oxidation and high acetaldehyde content make it difficult for many, but offer rewarding experiences to persistent explorers.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Sysco-ification of Wine
NEWSLETTERS.summ: How wine culture's lazy "drink what you like" ethos has failed, and why it's time to break out the pitchforks
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jason Wilson
takeaways: 71 percent of U.S. wine is controlled by the top 11 wine suppliers, according to 2024 American Association of Wine Economists data.
Gallo controlled 33 percent of the wine market in 2023 and has since acquired Rombauer, Hahn Family Wines, Massican, Whiny Baby, and Bev canned wine.
Independent wine distributors fell from roughly 3,000 in the 1990s to just over 1,000 today.
Three dominant distributors—Breakthru, RNDC, and Southern Glazer's—controlled 53 percent of wine and spirits distribution in 2024.
Popular U.S. restaurants typically feature identical mass-market wine lists from a handful of corporate wineries, creating an illusion of choice.
